title: South Carolina v. Katzenbach
summary: The Supreme Court upheld core provisions of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 as a valid exercise of Congress's enforcement power under the Fifteenth Amendment.
outcome_summary: The ruling protected the federal government's ability to block discriminatory voting rules in jurisdictions with entrenched anti-Black voter suppression.
impact_notes: A foundational Supreme Court decision supporting federal voting-rights enforcement.

scores.notes: Strongly reinforced the legal foundation of the Voting Rights Act.

title: Allen v. State Board of Elections
summary: The Supreme Court interpreted Section 5 of the Voting Rights Act broadly, allowing private suits and requiring federal review of a wider range of voting changes in covered jurisdictions.
outcome_summary: The decision expanded federal oversight of election law changes that could be used to dilute or suppress Black voting power.
impact_notes: A major early case broadening the practical reach of the Voting Rights Act.

scores.notes: Expanded the scope of Section 5 review and private enforcement.

sources.source_title: Allen v. State Board of Elections, 393 U.S. 544 (1969)

title: Shelby County v. Holder
summary: The Supreme Court invalidated the coverage formula used for federal preclearance under the Voting Rights Act.
outcome_summary: Weakened federal oversight of voting changes in jurisdictions with histories of racial discrimination.
impact_notes: Widely viewed as a major rollback of voting-rights enforcement.

title: Brnovich v. Democratic National Committee
summary: The Supreme Court upheld Arizona voting restrictions and adopted a narrower interpretation of Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act.
outcome_summary: Made it harder to challenge voting rules under the Voting Rights Act and was widely seen as another setback for minority voting-rights protections.
impact_notes: A modern voting-rights rollback that narrowed practical Section 2 protections.
